stem: A company stores sensitive files in S3 and wants to ensure all objects are encrypted at rest. They want to use a key that they manage themselves with the ability to audit every use of the key. Which S3 encryption option meets this requirement?

options.label: A
options.text: SSE-KMS using a customer managed key (CMK) in AWS KMS provides S3 encryption at rest with full control over key policy, rotation, and CloudTrail-audited key usage logging.

options.label: B
options.text: SSE-S3 uses an AWS managed key that is automatically rotated and provides strong AES-256 encryption, but the customer has no visibility into individual key usage.

options.label: C
options.text: SSE-C (customer-provided keys) requires the customer to supply the encryption key in each API request — AWS does not store the key, providing the highest key control but no audit trail.

options.label: D
options.text: Client-side encryption using the AWS Encryption SDK before uploading objects provides end-to-end encryption but does not integrate with AWS KMS for centralized key management.

distractors.B: SSE-S3 uses an AWS managed key that is automatically rotated and provides strong AES-256 encryption, but the customer has no visibility into individual key usage.
distractors.C: SSE-C (customer-provided keys) requires the customer to supply the encryption key in each API request — AWS does not store the key, providing the highest key control but no audit trail.
distractors.D: Client-side encryption using the AWS Encryption SDK before uploading objects provides end-to-end encryption but does not integrate with AWS KMS for centralized key management.

explanation: SSE-KMS with a customer managed key (CMK) is the recommended option for compliance-sensitive encryption. The CMK is created and managed by the customer in AWS KMS. Every use of the CMK (for generating data keys to encrypt/decrypt S3 objects) is logged in AWS CloudTrail, providing a complete audit trail of who accessed what data. You control the key policy, can enable automatic rotation, and can revoke access by changing the key policy. SSE-S3 uses AWS-managed keys with no customer visibility into usage.

stem: What is envelope encryption, and how does AWS KMS implement it for S3 data?

options.label: A
options.text: KMS generates a unique data encryption key (DEK) for each S3 object; the DEK encrypts the object; KMS then encrypts the DEK using the CMK — only the encrypted DEK is stored alongside the object, not the plaintext DEK.

options.label: B
options.text: Envelope encryption means the S3 object is encrypted twice — first with the CMK, then the ciphertext is encrypted again with a second DEK — creating a two-layer envelope for the data.

options.label: C
options.text: Envelope encryption uses the CMK directly to encrypt each S3 object — the key is the envelope that wraps the data, and the CMK never leaves KMS even during encryption.

options.label: D
options.text: KMS creates a key envelope by splitting the CMK into two halves — one stored in KMS and one stored with the encrypted S3 object — requiring both halves to decrypt the data.

distractors.B: Envelope encryption means the S3 object is encrypted twice — first with the CMK, then the ciphertext is encrypted again with a second DEK — creating a two-layer envelope for the data.
distractors.C: Envelope encryption uses the CMK directly to encrypt each S3 object — the key is the envelope that wraps the data, and the CMK never leaves KMS even during encryption.
distractors.D: KMS creates a key envelope by splitting the CMK into two halves — one stored in KMS and one stored with the encrypted S3 object — requiring both halves to decrypt the data.

explanation: Envelope encryption is a fundamental data protection pattern: (1) KMS generates a unique data encryption key (DEK) for each object; (2) the DEK encrypts the object (locally, without the data going through KMS); (3) KMS uses the CMK to encrypt the DEK; (4) the encrypted DEK is stored alongside the encrypted object in S3. To decrypt: S3 sends the encrypted DEK to KMS, KMS decrypts it using the CMK, and returns the plaintext DEK which decrypts the object. The CMK never directly touches the data — it only wraps/unwraps DEKs. This allows large data encryption without sending data through KMS.

stem: A company needs to ensure that sensitive data in S3 buckets — such as credit card numbers, SSNs, and email addresses — is automatically discovered and classified across thousands of buckets. Which AWS service provides this?

options.label: A
options.text: Amazon Macie uses machine learning to automatically discover and classify sensitive data (PII, financial data, credentials) in S3 buckets and generates findings for policy violations and sensitive data exposures.

options.label: B
options.text: Amazon Inspector scans S3 bucket contents for sensitive data patterns and generates findings for PII and financial data detected in objects stored across all configured buckets.

options.label: C
options.text: AWS Config with the s3-bucket-level-public-access-prohibited rule detects public access settings but does not scan object contents for sensitive data classification.

options.label: D
options.text: AWS Trusted Advisor analyzes S3 bucket permissions and flags buckets that likely contain sensitive data based on naming patterns and access patterns but does not scan object contents.

distractors.B: Amazon Inspector scans S3 bucket contents for sensitive data patterns and generates findings for PII and financial data detected in objects stored across all configured buckets.
distractors.C: AWS Config with the s3-bucket-level-public-access-prohibited rule detects public access settings but does not scan object contents for sensitive data classification.
distractors.D: AWS Trusted Advisor analyzes S3 bucket permissions and flags buckets that likely contain sensitive data based on naming patterns and access patterns but does not scan object contents.

explanation: Amazon Macie is the AWS managed data classification and data loss prevention service for S3. It uses machine learning and pattern matching to detect sensitive data types including PII (names, addresses, SSNs, passport numbers), financial information (credit cards, bank account numbers), credentials (API keys, private keys), and over 100 other data types. Macie also evaluates S3 bucket configurations and generates policy findings for overly permissive access settings. It is the only AWS service that performs content-level scanning of S3 objects.

stem: An application needs to store and retrieve a database password that must be automatically rotated every 30 days. Which AWS service natively supports automatic secret rotation?

options.label: A
options.text: AWS Secrets Manager supports automatic secret rotation using Lambda functions, with built-in integration for rotating credentials for RDS, Redshift, DocumentDB, and other services on a configurable schedule.

options.label: B
options.text: AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store SecureString supports automatic rotation on a configurable schedule by triggering a Lambda function whenever a parameter's expiry date is reached.

options.label: C
options.text: AWS Key Management Service supports automatic key rotation every 365 days for customer managed keys but does not store application credentials like database passwords.

options.label: D
options.text: Amazon Cognito User Pools support automatic password rotation for application users and service accounts, including database connections that use Cognito for authentication.

distractors.B: AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store SecureString supports automatic rotation on a configurable schedule by triggering a Lambda function whenever a parameter's expiry date is reached.
distractors.C: AWS Key Management Service supports automatic key rotation every 365 days for customer managed keys but does not store application credentials like database passwords.
distractors.D: Amazon Cognito User Pools support automatic password rotation for application users and service accounts, including database connections that use Cognito for authentication.

explanation: AWS Secrets Manager is purpose-built for secret rotation. It has built-in rotation Lambda functions for Amazon RDS (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, MariaDB), Amazon Redshift, Amazon DocumentDB, and Amazon ElastiCache. You configure rotation schedule (e.g., every 30 days), and Secrets Manager automatically calls the Lambda to update the credentials in both the secret store and the target database. Applications that retrieve credentials via the Secrets Manager API automatically get the new credentials after rotation. SSM Parameter Store does not have native rotation functionality.

stem: A company needs to protect a large number of sensitive financial records in DynamoDB. They want encryption at rest that allows them to control which entities can access the data by managing the KMS key policy. What should they use?

options.label: A
options.text: Enable DynamoDB encryption at rest with a customer managed KMS key — DynamoDB uses envelope encryption with the CMK, and you control access to the data by controlling who can use the key via the KMS key policy.

options.label: B
options.text: Enable DynamoDB encryption at rest with the DynamoDB owned key (default) — this provides the strongest encryption because AWS manages key rotation on a daily basis.

options.label: C
options.text: Use DynamoDB encryption at rest with an AWS managed key — AWS controls all key policies but provides a dedicated key per account that is separate from other services' keys.

options.label: D
options.text: Implement DynamoDB client-side encryption using the DynamoDB Encryption Client before writing items — this prevents AWS from accessing the plaintext data since encryption happens before the data is transmitted to DynamoDB.

distractors.B: Enable DynamoDB encryption at rest with the DynamoDB owned key (default) — this provides the strongest encryption because AWS manages key rotation on a daily basis.
distractors.C: Use DynamoDB encryption at rest with an AWS managed key — AWS controls all key policies but provides a dedicated key per account that is separate from other services' keys.
distractors.D: Implement DynamoDB client-side encryption using the DynamoDB Encryption Client before writing items — this prevents AWS from accessing the plaintext data since encryption happens before the data is transmitted to DynamoDB.

explanation: DynamoDB supports three encryption at rest options: (1) DynamoDB owned key (default) — free, managed by AWS, no visibility or control; (2) AWS managed key (aws/dynamodb) — customer can see usage in CloudTrail but cannot modify the key policy; (3) Customer managed key — you create and manage the KMS key, define the key policy to control which IAM entities can use it to access DynamoDB data, and can view all key usage in CloudTrail. For compliance requirements requiring data access control through key management, option 3 is required.

stem: An organization must ensure that all data transmitted between clients and an Application Load Balancer is encrypted. The security team also wants to enforce a minimum TLS version of 1.2. How is this enforced on an ALB?

options.label: A
options.text: Configure an HTTPS listener on the ALB and select a security policy (e.g., ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06) that specifies the minimum TLS version and allowed cipher suites.

options.label: B
options.text: Enable ALB access logging with TLS metadata — this enforces TLS 1.2 minimum by rejecting connections from clients that present a TLS 1.1 or lower handshake request.

options.label: C
options.text: Deploy a WAF Web ACL on the ALB and add a custom rule that blocks HTTP requests and requests using TLS versions below 1.2 based on the request's TLS metadata header.

options.label: D
options.text: Configure the ALB target group with HTTPS protocol and set the minimum TLS version in the target group health check settings to enforce TLS 1.2 for all connections.

distractors.B: Enable ALB access logging with TLS metadata — this enforces TLS 1.2 minimum by rejecting connections from clients that present a TLS 1.1 or lower handshake request.
distractors.C: Deploy a WAF Web ACL on the ALB and add a custom rule that blocks HTTP requests and requests using TLS versions below 1.2 based on the request's TLS metadata header.
distractors.D: Configure the ALB target group with HTTPS protocol and set the minimum TLS version in the target group health check settings to enforce TLS 1.2 for all connections.

explanation: ALB security policies control which TLS protocol versions and cipher suites are accepted on HTTPS listeners. AWS provides predefined security policies like ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06, which supports TLS 1.2 and 1.3 and disables older versions and weak ciphers. You select the policy on the listener configuration. Clients connecting with TLS 1.1 or older will have their connection refused during the TLS handshake. WAF operates on HTTP/HTTPS application layer content after the TLS handshake completes and cannot affect the TLS negotiation itself.

stem: A security engineer is asked to generate an asymmetric key pair for signing JWTs that an application will verify. The private key must never leave AWS. Which AWS service enables this?

options.label: A
options.text: AWS KMS supports asymmetric key pairs (RSA and ECC) for signing and verification — the private key never leaves KMS, and signing operations are performed within KMS by calling the Sign API.

options.label: B
options.text: AWS Certificate Manager generates asymmetric key pairs for TLS certificates where the private key is stored in AWS, but ACM keys cannot be used for custom application signing operations.

options.label: C
options.text: AWS Secrets Manager stores asymmetric key pairs generated externally, providing a secure vault where the private key is encrypted at rest and never transmitted in plaintext.

options.label: D
options.text: Amazon Cognito User Pools generate RSA key pairs for JWT signing automatically — the private key is managed by Cognito and can be exported for use in custom signing operations.

distractors.B: AWS Certificate Manager generates asymmetric key pairs for TLS certificates where the private key is stored in AWS, but ACM keys cannot be used for custom application signing operations.
distractors.C: AWS Secrets Manager stores asymmetric key pairs generated externally, providing a secure vault where the private key is encrypted at rest and never transmitted in plaintext.
distractors.D: Amazon Cognito User Pools generate RSA key pairs for JWT signing automatically — the private key is managed by Cognito and can be exported for use in custom signing operations.

explanation: AWS KMS supports asymmetric cryptography with RSA (2048, 3072, 4096 bit) and ECC (P-256, P-384, secp256k1) key pairs. For signing, the private key is created in and never leaves KMS hardware. Applications call the `kms:Sign` API with the message and receive the signature. Verifiers can use the public key (obtained via `kms:GetPublicKey`) locally without calling KMS for every verification. This is ideal for JWT signing, document signing, and other asymmetric operations where the private key must remain hardware-protected.

stem: A company needs Hardware Security Module (HSM) level key control for cryptographic operations, where only they — not AWS — control the HSM and can access the keys. Which service provides this?

options.label: A
options.text: AWS CloudHSM provides dedicated FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated HSMs within the customer's VPC — AWS manages the hardware but the customer controls the HSM and all keys; AWS cannot access the cryptographic material.

options.label: B
options.text: AWS KMS with customer managed keys provides FIPS 140-2 Level 2 validated key storage where AWS manages the HSMs but customers control key policies — equivalent to CloudHSM for most compliance needs.

options.label: C
options.text: AWS Secrets Manager with KMS encryption provides hardware-level key protection using dedicated HSMs, giving customers exclusive control of their keys and cryptographic operations.

options.label: D
options.text: AWS Private CA backed by CloudHSM provides dedicated HSMs for certificate authority operations, but the CA private key remains under AWS control as part of the managed service.

distractors.B: AWS KMS with customer managed keys provides FIPS 140-2 Level 2 validated key storage where AWS manages the HSMs but customers control key policies — equivalent to CloudHSM for most compliance needs.
distractors.C: AWS Secrets Manager with KMS encryption provides hardware-level key protection using dedicated HSMs, giving customers exclusive control of their keys and cryptographic operations.
distractors.D: AWS Private CA backed by CloudHSM provides dedicated HSMs for certificate authority operations, but the CA private key remains under AWS control as part of the managed service.

explanation: AWS CloudHSM deploys physical HSMs (FIPS 140-2 Level 3) in the customer's VPC, accessible only within the VPC. Unlike KMS, CloudHSM is single-tenant — the HSM is not shared with other customers. More importantly, AWS has no access to the cryptographic material or HSM admin credentials — the customer initializes the HSM and holds all credentials. KMS uses shared FIPS 140-2 Level 2 HSMs where AWS manages the hardware and has administrative access. CloudHSM is required when compliance mandates require the customer to exclusively hold the HSM keys.

stem: An S3 bucket stores regulatory records that must be immutable — once written, they cannot be modified or deleted for 7 years. Which S3 feature enforces this requirement?

options.label: A
options.text: S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode with a 7-year retention period makes objects write-once-read-many (WORM) and prevents modification or deletion by any user, including root, during the retention period.

options.label: B
options.text: S3 Versioning preserves all versions of objects when overwritten or deleted — by enabling versioning and disabling delete markers, objects effectively become immutable for the retention period.

options.label: C
options.text: S3 Replication with delete marker replication disabled ensures that objects are copied to a destination bucket and cannot be deleted in the source without also affecting the replica.

options.label: D
options.text: S3 server-side encryption with a KMS key that has a deletion-pending status prevents any modification of encrypted objects, creating effective immutability for the retention period.

distractors.B: S3 Versioning preserves all versions of objects when overwritten or deleted — by enabling versioning and disabling delete markers, objects effectively become immutable for the retention period.
distractors.C: S3 Replication with delete marker replication disabled ensures that objects are copied to a destination bucket and cannot be deleted in the source without also affecting the replica.
distractors.D: S3 server-side encryption with a KMS key that has a deletion-pending status prevents any modification of encrypted objects, creating effective immutability for the retention period.

explanation: S3 Object Lock provides WORM (Write Once Read Many) storage. In Compliance mode, the locked retention period cannot be shortened or the lock mode changed by any user — including the root account and AWS Support. In Governance mode, authorized users with special permissions can bypass the lock. S3 Versioning alone does not prevent deletion; MFA Delete adds friction but doesn't enforce retention periods. Object Lock in Compliance mode is the only AWS mechanism that provides truly enforced immutability against all users for a defined period.

stem: A company wants to issue private SSL/TLS certificates for internal services (not publicly trusted) without the overhead of managing certificate authority infrastructure. Which AWS service provides a managed private CA?

options.label: A
options.text: AWS Private Certificate Authority (AWS Private CA) is a managed service for creating and operating private CAs — issue internal certificates without managing CA hardware, software, or revocation infrastructure.

options.label: B
options.text: AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) issues both public and private certificates for AWS services — private certificates are stored securely in ACM and can be used for internal services without a separate CA.

options.label: C
options.text: AWS Secrets Manager stores private CA certificates and private keys, enabling centralized management of internal certificate issuance using stored CA credentials.

options.label: D
options.text: Amazon Route 53 includes a private CA feature that issues certificates for domains hosted in private hosted zones, automatically handling certificate renewal and revocation.

distractors.B: AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) issues both public and private certificates for AWS services — private certificates are stored securely in ACM and can be used for internal services without a separate CA.
distractors.C: AWS Secrets Manager stores private CA certificates and private keys, enabling centralized management of internal certificate issuance using stored CA credentials.
distractors.D: Amazon Route 53 includes a private CA feature that issues certificates for domains hosted in private hosted zones, automatically handling certificate renewal and revocation.

explanation: AWS Private CA (formerly ACM Private CA) is a managed CA service. You create a root or subordinate CA hierarchy in AWS without managing servers or CA software. Private CA issues certificates signed by your private root and handles CRL and OCSP revocation. ACM can use a Private CA to issue certificates that auto-renew and deploy to AWS services. Private CA supports custom certificate templates, X.509 extensions, and integration with AWS Organizations for cross-account certificate issuance. ACM itself issues public certificates (validated by public CAs) and can issue private certificates only when integrated with Private CA.

stem: A company stores EBS volumes containing regulated data. A security audit requires that all EBS volumes be encrypted and that the encryption keys be customer managed. How can the team enforce this across all new EC2 instances?

options.label: A
options.text: Enable EBS encryption by default in the EC2 settings for each Region, specifying a customer managed KMS key — all new EBS volumes and snapshots created in that Region are automatically encrypted with that key.

options.label: B
options.text: Create an AWS Config rule requiring EBS encryption and use auto-remediation to encrypt any unencrypted volumes after creation — this prevents unencrypted data from persisting.

options.label: C
options.text: Use an SCP to deny ec2:CreateVolume actions where the encryption flag is false — this prevents any account from creating unencrypted EBS volumes across the organization.

options.label: D
options.text: Deploy a CloudFormation template that creates all EC2 instances with encrypted volumes — requiring all EC2 provisioning to use CloudFormation enforces encryption consistently.

distractors.B: Create an AWS Config rule requiring EBS encryption and use auto-remediation to encrypt any unencrypted volumes after creation — this prevents unencrypted data from persisting.
distractors.C: Use an SCP to deny ec2:CreateVolume actions where the encryption flag is false — this prevents any account from creating unencrypted EBS volumes across the organization.
distractors.D: Deploy a CloudFormation template that creates all EC2 instances with encrypted volumes — requiring all EC2 provisioning to use CloudFormation enforces encryption consistently.

explanation: EC2 has an account-level 'EBS encryption by default' setting per Region. When enabled, all new EBS volumes — whether created standalone or as part of EC2 instance launches, and all snapshots copied into the Region — are automatically encrypted. You specify a default KMS key (which can be a customer managed key). This is the most comprehensive preventive control. Config auto-remediation is reactive and may not apply before data is written. SCP conditions on volume creation with specific parameters are complex to implement correctly.

stem: A company wants to prevent sensitive data in S3 from being accidentally made public. They want a single control that blocks all public access to S3 buckets across the entire organization. What is the simplest way to enforce this?

options.label: A
options.text: Enable S3 Block Public Access at the account level for all accounts in the organization using AWS Organizations and AWS Config — this prevents any bucket from being made publicly accessible.

options.label: B
options.text: Apply an SCP that denies s3:PutBucketAcl and s3:PutBucketPolicy API calls — this prevents administrators from creating public bucket policies or ACLs across the organization.

options.label: C
options.text: Use Amazon Macie to automatically detect when S3 buckets become public and trigger a Lambda function to reverse the public access configuration immediately.

options.label: D
options.text: Configure AWS Config with the s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited and s3-bucket-public-write-prohibited managed rules and enable auto-remediation on all accounts.

distractors.B: Apply an SCP that denies s3:PutBucketAcl and s3:PutBucketPolicy API calls — this prevents administrators from creating public bucket policies or ACLs across the organization.
distractors.C: Use Amazon Macie to automatically detect when S3 buckets become public and trigger a Lambda function to reverse the public access configuration immediately.
distractors.D: Configure AWS Config with the s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited and s3-bucket-public-write-prohibited managed rules and enable auto-remediation on all accounts.

explanation: S3 Block Public Access is a single setting that can be applied at the account level (or bucket level) to prevent any bucket in that account from becoming publicly accessible. When enabled at the account level, it overrides any bucket ACL or bucket policy that grants public access — regardless of what those policies say. Via AWS Organizations and account-level enforcement, you can ensure all accounts have this setting active. This is simpler and more reliable than SCP-based or Config-based approaches, which may not catch all paths to public access.

stem: A KMS customer managed key needs to be rotated. A security engineer enables automatic key rotation. What actually happens when KMS performs automatic rotation?

options.label: A
options.text: KMS creates new backing key material for the CMK on the rotation schedule — the CMK's key ID, ARN, and alias remain the same, so no application changes are needed, and KMS retains old material to decrypt previously encrypted data.

options.label: B
options.text: KMS creates a completely new CMK with a new key ID and ARN, automatically updating all policies and aliases that referenced the old key to point to the new one.

options.label: C
options.text: KMS exports the current key material to S3, generates new key material, re-encrypts all data encrypted with the old key, and then deletes the old key material from HSMs.

options.label: D
options.text: KMS rotates the key by incrementing the key version number — applications must explicitly specify the new version in API calls to use the rotated key for new encryption operations.

distractors.B: KMS creates a completely new CMK with a new key ID and ARN, automatically updating all policies and aliases that referenced the old key to point to the new one.
distractors.C: KMS exports the current key material to S3, generates new key material, re-encrypts all data encrypted with the old key, and then deletes the old key material from HSMs.
distractors.D: KMS rotates the key by incrementing the key version number — applications must explicitly specify the new version in API calls to use the rotated key for new encryption operations.

explanation: KMS automatic key rotation (available for symmetric CMKs) works transparently: KMS generates new backing key material annually (or on your schedule with on-demand rotation) but the key ID, ARN, and aliases all remain unchanged. Existing ciphertexts encrypted with old key material can still be decrypted because KMS retains all previous backing key versions. New encryption operations use the latest key material. No application changes are required. KMS does NOT re-encrypt existing data — that would require a separate re-encryption operation by the data owner.

stem: A security engineer wants to ensure that data stored in RDS is encrypted at rest and that the database can only be accessed over encrypted connections. How are both requirements enforced?

options.label: A
options.text: Enable RDS encryption at rest (KMS) at DB instance creation time, and enforce SSL/TLS connections by setting the `rds.force_ssl` parameter to 1 in the DB parameter group.

options.label: B
options.text: Enable RDS Multi-AZ for encryption at rest — Multi-AZ automatically enables KMS encryption for the primary and standby — and restrict the security group to HTTPS port 443 for all connections.

options.label: C
options.text: Use RDS Proxy in front of the database — RDS Proxy encrypts all connections to the database at rest and in transit without requiring changes to the database instance configuration.

options.label: D
options.text: Create a custom RDS parameter group with encryption_at_rest=enabled and require_ssl=true, then apply both parameters to the existing DB instance without requiring a restart.

distractors.B: Enable RDS Multi-AZ for encryption at rest — Multi-AZ automatically enables KMS encryption for the primary and standby — and restrict the security group to HTTPS port 443 for all connections.
distractors.C: Use RDS Proxy in front of the database — RDS Proxy encrypts all connections to the database at rest and in transit without requiring changes to the database instance configuration.
distractors.D: Create a custom RDS parameter group with encryption_at_rest=enabled and require_ssl=true, then apply both parameters to the existing DB instance without requiring a restart.

explanation: For RDS security: (1) Encryption at rest must be enabled at instance creation time — it cannot be enabled on an existing unencrypted instance (you must create a snapshot, encrypt the copy, and restore). KMS encrypts the underlying storage, automated backups, read replicas, and snapshots. (2) Enforce SSL/TLS by setting the engine-specific SSL parameter: `rds.force_ssl=1` for PostgreSQL/SQL Server, `require_secure_transport=ON` for MySQL. This causes the DB engine to reject unencrypted connection attempts. Multi-AZ does not enable encryption — they are independent settings.

stem: A developer accidentally committed an AWS KMS CMK key ID (not the actual key material) to a public GitHub repository. What is the security risk and what action should be taken?

options.label: A
options.text: The key ID alone is not sensitive — the actual risk is low because the key ID without appropriate IAM permissions cannot decrypt data or invoke KMS operations. No emergency remediation is needed but review IAM access.

options.label: B
options.text: The key ID is equivalent to the key material — anyone who finds it can use it to decrypt data encrypted by that CMK without requiring IAM permissions. The CMK must be immediately deleted.

options.label: C
options.text: The key ID should be treated as sensitive because KMS automatically grants Decrypt permissions to any principal that presents a valid key ID, bypassing normal IAM authorization.

options.label: D
options.text: Rotate the CMK immediately to generate a new key ID that invalidates the exposed key ID — KMS key rotation changes both the backing material and the key ID for security.

distractors.B: The key ID is equivalent to the key material — anyone who finds it can use it to decrypt data encrypted by that CMK without requiring IAM permissions. The CMK must be immediately deleted.
distractors.C: The key ID should be treated as sensitive because KMS automatically grants Decrypt permissions to any principal that presents a valid key ID, bypassing normal IAM authorization.
distractors.D: Rotate the CMK immediately to generate a new key ID that invalidates the exposed key ID — KMS key rotation changes both the backing material and the key ID for security.

explanation: A KMS key ID (e.g., `mrk-abc123...` or a UUID) is a public identifier, similar to an ARN. Knowing the key ID gives an attacker no ability to use the key — every KMS API call (Encrypt, Decrypt, GenerateDataKey) requires the caller to have explicit IAM permissions. The key ID alone does not grant any cryptographic capability. However, the key ID can be useful in reconnaissance (confirming the key exists, identifying its type/usage from CloudTrail). The real risk would be if access keys were committed — review if any IAM credentials were also exposed.

stem: A security engineer needs to provide a third-party auditor with access to SSL/TLS certificates, cryptographic standards compliance reports, and other security documentation for an AWS environment. Which service provides this?

options.label: A
options.text: AWS Artifact provides on-demand access to AWS compliance reports, certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2, PCI DSS), and agreements — auditors can download these documents directly from the Artifact console.

options.label: B
options.text: AWS Security Hub provides downloadable compliance reports for all security standards it monitors, including TLS certificate details and cryptographic configuration summaries.

options.label: C
options.text: AWS Config Conformance Packs generate downloadable compliance reports showing the security configuration state of all resources against specified standards for third-party review.

options.label: D
options.text: Amazon Inspector generates compliance reports covering SSL/TLS certificate status, cipher suite configurations, and infrastructure security posture suitable for third-party auditors.

distractors.B: AWS Security Hub provides downloadable compliance reports for all security standards it monitors, including TLS certificate details and cryptographic configuration summaries.
distractors.C: AWS Config Conformance Packs generate downloadable compliance reports showing the security configuration state of all resources against specified standards for third-party review.
distractors.D: Amazon Inspector generates compliance reports covering SSL/TLS certificate status, cipher suite configurations, and infrastructure security posture suitable for third-party auditors.

explanation: AWS Artifact is the self-service portal for accessing AWS compliance documentation. It provides: AWS audit reports (SOC 1, SOC 2, SOC 3), certifications (ISO 27001, ISO 27017, ISO 27018, PCI DSS AOC), FIPS 140-2 validation certificates, and agreements such as BAA (Business Associate Agreement for HIPAA) and NDA. Customers can share these reports with auditors to support their own compliance programs. This is the only AWS service providing official third-party audit reports and attestations from AWS itself.
